fields.definition_origin: Clark Hull (1932, 1934) — 'The goal-gradient hypothesis and maze learning' (Psychological Review): rats accelerate as they near a food reward; effort increases as a function of proximity to goal. Re-applied to human consumer behavior by Kivetz, Urminsky & Zheng (2006) 'The Goal-Gradient Hypothesis Resurrected: Purchase Acceleration, Illusionary Goal Progress, and Customer Retention' (Journal of Marketing Research, XLIII:39-58) — coffee-card and online-rating field experiments demonstrated the effect, plus introduced 'illusionary' / 'endowed' progress (artificial head start) doubling completion rates. Onboarding application by NN/g and Material 3 Expressive motion guidelines.
fields.core_mechanism: Motivation is non-linear with respect to distance-to-goal: effort accelerates as the goal becomes proximate. Two distinct sub-mechanisms. (1) Goal-gradient proper: a user 6/8 done is more motivated than a user 1/8 done — completion energy compounds. (2) Endowed progress (Kivetz 2006): an artificial head start ('Step 2 of 8' rather than 'Step 1 of 7') reframes the task as already in-progress and disproportionately increases completion likelihood. The two combine: progress indicators on multi-step onboarding both visualize approach (firing the gradient) and embed endowment (starting at 2 of N, not 0 of N-1). Crucially the indicator must show motion within ~200ms of input — delayed feedback breaks the 'I'm making progress' loop. The indicator also functions as a Cognitive Load reducer: it externalizes 'where am I' from working memory.
fields.neuroscience_correlate: Goal-gradient effects engage ventral striatum reward anticipation (Knutson et al. 2001) — proximity to reward fires anticipatory dopamine signaling, which is itself reinforcing. Endowed progress likely engages medial PFC self-referential processing (the user encodes the partially-completed task as 'mine'). Progress feedback at <200ms latency falls within the perceptual present, registering as causal cause-and-effect (Michotte 1946); slower feedback breaks the causal binding. Hippocampal sequence binding maintains step-position memory.
fields.empirical_evidence: (1) Hull (1932) original maze studies — effect size large, replicated across rodent learning literature for decades. (2) Kivetz, Urminsky & Zheng (2006) — coffee-card field study (n=450 customers): cards starting with 2 stamps already given (10-stamp card vs equivalent 8-stamp blank card) had ~30% higher completion rate; online-rating experiment confirmed effect. (3) Nunes & Drèze (2006) 'The Endowed Progress Effect' (Journal of Consumer Research) — replicated and extended. (4) Amir & Ariely (2008) — endowed-progress effect held in online task settings. (5) Industry validation: Mailchimp, Stripe Atlas, Robinhood progress bars cited in NN/g. Multi-step flows with progress indicators yield materially higher completion than indicator-less equivalents (NN/g 2018 meta of usability tests, ~10-15% lift). (6) Kleeve PDF research base claim: multi-step progress bars yield 'materially higher completion' (specific %s vary by app/category). (7) Long-onboarding case: Cal AI 33-step uses sub-progress chunked indicators ('Section 2 of 4 / Step 5 of 8') reducing fatigue.
fields.measurable_outcome_metric: Step-by-step completion rate (look for inverse curve where final-third completion > middle-third), questionnaire completion rate overall, time-on-step distribution as user approaches end (should DECREASE per Hull), abandonment-by-step ratio, A/B test of with-progress vs without-progress indicator (expect 10-30% lift on completion).
fields.conversion_lift_benchmark: Kivetz et al. (2006) coffee-card study: ~30% completion lift on endowed cards vs equivalent blank cards. Nunes & Drèze (2006): replicated. Industry meta (NN/g 2018): multi-step progress indicators yield ~10-15% higher completion than indicator-less flows. Endowed progress in onboarding A/B tests (Mailchimp, public case studies): 5-20% completion lift. Cal AI's 33-step chunked progress: completion rate >60% on a 33-step questionnaire (industry-leading for that depth).
fields.engineering_tactics: (1) Progress bar at the top of every multi-step screen, fixed-height (4dp), full-width-minus-32dp-padding, brand color fill on completed portion, light-grey track. (2) Endowed progress: start the count at 2 of N (not 1 of N-1). For chunked flows, show 'Section 1 of 4 — Step 2 of 8' nesting. (3) Motion within 200ms of user input — animate progress fill on Continue tap with Reanimated 3 timing(duration: 350) — long enough to be visible, short enough to feel responsive. (4) Per-step visual reward: the bar fills with brand color, sensoryFeedback .selection on tap reinforces. (5) Avoid fake progress: bar fill speed must feel proportional to step count (not 12.5% jumps that don't match perceived effort). (6) For long flows (15+ steps): chunked sub-progress with section labels ('Section 2 of 4: About you'). (7) State persistence: progress state stored in MMKV + server, survives app-kill / reinstall (cross-device resume hydration); resume from where left off, keep gradient intact. (8) Final-step amplification: at step N-1 (the AI plan reveal), the progress bar visually completes — this is a perceptual peak embedded in the bar itself.
fields.real_world_examples: (1) Cal AI 33-step questionnaire — chunked progress 'Section 2 of 4', 'Step 5 of 8' (endowed start), final fill at step 33 = peak. (2) Duolingo's lesson progress bar — green fill across 5-question lesson, animates per-answer; the final question's fill triggers celebration. (3) Stripe Atlas onboarding — top-of-page progress with 5 chunked sections, endowed at section 1 with 'Verify' already checked from email. (4) Mailchimp campaign builder — 4-step horizontal stepper, endowed progress (account info pre-filled). (5) Headway book-summary onboarding — 8-step linear progress bar, ~12% width per step. (6) Fitbod fitness onboarding — 6-step progress with section labels, endowed start. (7) Anti-example: a 12-step onboarding with NO progress bar — abandonment compounds at step 4-5 because users don't know 'how much more'. (8) Anti-example: progress percentage with arbitrary precision ('87% complete') — feels artificial; round to step counts.
fields.mobile_vs_web_diffs: Mobile progress indicators must be top-anchored (thumb doesn't obscure), 4dp height (not too prominent — competes with content), brand color fill animated 350ms. Web progress indicators can be larger and contain step labels (more screen real estate). Mobile uses chunked indicators more aggressively because viewport is small. Web equivalent: linear stepper with step-name labels, often 5-step horizontal at top of page (Stripe Atlas pattern). Mobile sometimes uses vertical step indicators for in-context (chat-like) onboarding.
fields.platform_design_language_alignment: Aligned with both. Material 3 Expressive (I/O 2025) treats progress motion springs as first-class primitives — its expressive shape system + per-step animation are ideal for goal-gradient. Apple Liquid Glass (iOS 26+) — progress fills with translucent material + dynamic morph create the visceral 'I'm advancing' feel. Both platforms ship-faster guidance: don't fight the system progress component (UIProgressView, LinearProgressIndicator); style it within constraints.
fields.category_fit: Universal for any multi-step onboarding. Critical for: long-questionnaire wellness/habit (Cal AI, Flo, Noom — without progress bar, abandonment spikes), edtech (Duolingo, Headway), fintech KYC (multi-step verification needs gradient), B2B trial setup (Linear, Notion). Less critical: single-screen onboarding (Instagram, Acorns) where there's no gradient to fire. DO NOT use progress indicators that lie about progress (decorative bars unlinked to actual completion) — DFA dark-pattern risk.
fields.tooling_options: Native: UIProgressView (iOS), LinearProgressIndicator (Android Material 3). React Native: Reanimated 3 + react-native-svg for custom progress, react-native-paper LinearProgressIndicator for M3 default. State: XState v5 onboarding FSM exposes step / total counts to the progress component. Persistence: MMKV client + Postgres server. Analytics: PostHog step-by-step funnel. For chunked progress: bespoke component with section + step props.
fields.ai_dependency: none: progress indicators are static design + state; no LLM call required. Optional: small-model classifier in CI to flag onboarding flows missing progress indicators or starting at 'Step 1 of N' (no endowed progress).
fields.claude_design_application: Instruct Claude to wire progress as endowed-gradient + responsive motion. Example fragment 1: 'Build the OnboardingProgress component for the 12-step questionnaire. Hard rules: (a) fixed-position top, 4dp tall, full-width-minus-32dp; (b) brand-color fill on completed portion, neutral-100 track; (c) endowed: start at \'Step 2 of 13\' on first render (not \'Step 1 of 12\'); (d) animate fill on step-change with Reanimated 3 timing(350ms, easing: Easing.bezier(0.4, 0, 0.2, 1)); (e) sensoryFeedback .selection on Continue tap; (f) chunked section label below bar in 12pt Manrope 500 (\'Section 2 of 4 — About you\'); (g) final step (13/13) triggers full-fill animation as part of the AI plan reveal peak. Persist current step to MMKV with key onboarding_progress.step.' Example fragment 2: 'Audit the onboarding state machine: every step must increment progress, the progress bar must show animation within 200ms of user input, and progress state must survive app-kill (MMKV persistence + server hydration on auth). Output a step → progress-fraction table; reject any flow without endowed start.'
fields.stitch_application: Stitch must be told this is endowed + chunked, not flat. Example fragment 1: 'Mobile onboarding screen with top progress bar. Bar position: 16dp from top safe-area, 32dp left/right padding, 4dp tall, 2px corner radius. Track color: light grey #E5E7EB. Fill color: brand orange #FF6B47. Fill width: 23% on screen 3 of 13 (endowed start at step 2 of 13). Below bar: 12pt Manrope 500 label \'Section 1 of 4 — About you\' in muted grey. Above bar in safe area: tiny step counter \'3 of 13\' aligned right. Forbid percentage indicator with arbitrary precision (e.g. \'23.07% complete\').' Example fragment 2: 'For chunked sections: each section transition triggers a brief celebration micro-moment (200ms scale-in on the section label, sensoryFeedback .selection). Final step (13 of 13) fills the bar fully and the bar transitions into the AI plan reveal peak animation.'
fields.ai_codegen_failure_mode: AI codegen defaults to: (1) progress bar starting at 'Step 1 of N' (no endowed progress); (2) flat percentage indicator with weird precision ('23.07%'); (3) no animation on step change (instant snap, breaks goal-gradient); (4) progress bar that doesn't persist (refresh = bar resets); (5) no chunked sections on long flows (33 flat steps with no narrative grouping); (6) no ARIA / VoiceOver announcement on step change. Counter-prompt: 'Endowed progress is mandatory: start at \'Step 2 of N\', not \'Step 1 of N-1\'. Animate progress fill on step change (Reanimated 3 timing 350ms). Persist progress state to MMKV + server. For flows >10 steps, chunk into sections with labels (\'Section 2 of 4\'). Forbid percentage indicators with arbitrary precision. VoiceOver announces \'Step N of M, percent complete\' on each step change.'
fields.anti_patterns: (1) Decorative progress bar unlinked to actual completion (DFA dark-pattern risk). (2) 'Step 1 of N-1' (missing endowed-progress lift). (3) Progress percentage with arbitrary precision ('87% complete'). (4) Progress bar that resets on app-kill / refresh (destroys gradient). (5) Progress indicator only on the last screen (defeats purpose). (6) Bar fill speed disproportionate to actual effort (12.5% jumps on a step that took 30 seconds). (7) No motion on step change (snap-to-position breaks the perceptual present). (8) Progress positioned behind keyboard or under thumb (invisible during input). (9) Progress shown in tiny text at top — must be visible at glance. (10) Mobile-flat 33-step with no section chunking (Cognitive Load + abandonment compound).
fields.regulatory_risk: Low-Medium. Decorative-fake progress bars (showing 'almost done' regardless of true state) are dark-pattern territory under EU Digital Fairness Act (Q4 2026 draft) and DSA. FTC has prosecuted fake-progress patterns in trial-cancellation flows. Mitigate: ensure progress accurately reflects step / total. Endowed progress (start at 2 of N, not 1 of N-1) is acceptable because the total still reflects actual remaining work; users complete the same workload in the same number of steps.
fields.platform_constraints_2026: WCAG 2.2 §1.4.13 Content on Hover or Focus — progress label must remain visible. Apple HIG explicitly recommends progress indicators on multi-step flows. Material 3 Expressive treats progress motion as first-class — use M3 LinearProgressIndicator. Apple Guideline 4.0 — design must be polished; sloppy progress indicators have been cited in rejections.
fields.accessibility_implications: Progress bar must be VoiceOver/TalkBack readable as 'Step N of M' on each change (set accessibilityValue or contentDescription). prefers-reduced-motion respect — replace 350ms spring fill with crossfade. Progress motion must not flash >3x/sec (WCAG 2.2 §2.3.1). Touch targets near progress (Continue / Back buttons) ≥44×44pt iOS / 48dp Android. High-contrast mode: ensure fill color contrasts with track at 3:1 minimum (WCAG 2.2 §1.4.11). Dynamic-type respect — chunked-section labels must scale with text size.
fields.pitfalls_caveats: (1) Endowed progress is most effective on first encounter; reinstall users see through it. (2) Long flows (33+ steps) fatigue users despite endowed progress — must chunk into sections. (3) Mismatch between step count and bar fill (e.g., 20% fill on step 7 of 13) breaks trust. (4) Performance: animating progress on low-end Android can drop frames; use Reanimated 3 worklets, not JS-thread Animated. (5) Network-flap: if progress state is server-source-of-truth and network drops, ensure local cache continues — don't freeze the bar. (6) AI plan reveal must NOT show its own progress bar overlapping the questionnaire bar — visual conflict. (7) Cultural calibration: linear progress is universal; section names need localization.
fields.cross_cultural_validity: Goal-gradient is biologically grounded (reward anticipation circuitry) and replicates universally. Endowed progress effect (Kivetz 2006) replicated cross-culturally in retail studies (US, EU). Cultural calibration: linear LTR progress bars must mirror for RTL (AR, HE) — fill direction reverses. Section labels must localize (not just translate — 'About you' may need different framing in JP/KR for politeness register). Visual language for completion is universal; copy is not. Numerical step counters are culturally neutral. CN/JP markets tolerate denser progress UIs (multiple bars + percentage + step count) without overload.
fields.prescribed_action: Wire progress as endowed-gradient: every multi-step onboarding shows a top progress bar starting at 'Step 2 of N' (not 'Step 1 of N-1'), animating fill within 350ms of Continue tap, persisting state to MMKV + server, and chunking into sections for flows >10 steps. The final step's full-fill animation must be wired into the AI plan reveal peak. Forbid percentage indicators with arbitrary precision and progress bars that don't survive app-kill.
fields.questionnaire_relevance: Directly determines questionnaire pacing perception. Progress bar is non-negotiable for any questionnaire >3 steps. Endowed start ('Step 2 of N') compounds with sunk-cost (item 4) — the user feels both 'I've already started' AND 'I've already invested', accelerating completion. Chunked sections on long questionnaires (>10 steps) reduce fatigue and let the goal-gradient fire repeatedly per section, not just once at end.
fields.aha_relevance: The progress bar's final fill at step N-1 is a perceptual peak baked into the indicator itself. The user's eye sees 'almost done' while the AI plan reveal peak is loading — the gradient firing primes the brain for the reward, intensifying the Aha when reveal lands. Without the gradient, the reveal lands in a flatter emotional state.
fields.emotional_archetype_alignment: Universal — every archetype benefits from clear progress. Strongest fit: Hero (mastery proximity), Sage (knowledge accumulation visualized), Magician (transformation step-count). Caregiver: progress should be gentle, not pressuring ('Step 3 of 8' rather than 'Hurry, only 5 left!'). Pairs with .agent/research/emotional-design/results/Jungian_Brand_Archetypes.json. Conflicts with Outlaw register slightly — rule-breaking voice doesn't sit well with rigid step counts; consider chunked sections only for Outlaw.
fields.anti_pattern_paired: Wall-of-Text + No Progress (deck-of-cards carousel that doesn't show step count); Wasted Loading Screens (the loader at peak should NOT show another progress bar — visual conflict); Decision Paralysis (5+ plan paywall after a fast progress bar leaves users disoriented); Decorative-Fake Progress (DFA dark pattern).
sources: [Kivetz, Urminsky & Zheng (2006) — The Goal-Gradient Hypothesis Resurrected (PDF)](https://www0.gsb.columbia.edu/mygsb/faculty/research/pubfiles/2275/Kivetz_Urminsky_Zheng_2006_JMR.pdf)
[Knutson et al. (2001) — Anticipation of monetary reward selectively recruits nucleus accumbens](https://www.jneurosci.org/content/21/16/RC159)
